introduction.p: The vector calculus projects ended with two dialects. In one dialect, we used vector fields, dot products, curls, divergences, and normal vectors. In the other, we used forms: 1\text{-forms},\qquad 2\text{-forms},\qquad 3\text{-forms}, together with the exterior derivative d.

These projects ask you to practice that translation. A successful project should make the reader feel that Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem, and the divergence theorem are not three unrelated miracles. They are one boundary principle seen in different dimensions.

subsection.title: Project 1: translate a classical vector calculus problem into forms
subsection.p: A classical vector calculus problem often begins with a vector field: \mathbf F=(P,Q) \qquad\text{or}\qquad \mathbf F=(P,Q,R). A forms solution begins by asking what kind of integral we are doing.
For flux through a surface, we use a 2-form: \boxed{\Phi_{\mathbf F} = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy.}
The same vector field may produce different forms, depending on what we are measuring.

A seed example: circulation around the unit circle. Let \mathbf F(x,y)=(-y,x). Classically, the work around a closed curve C is \oint_C \mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r. The corresponding 1-form is \boxed{\omega=-y\,dx+x\,dy.}

Now use Green's theorem in forms language. Compute
d\omega \amp = d(-y\,dx+x\,dy) \amp = 2\,dx\wedge dy.
If D is the unit disk, then
\int_D d\omega \amp = \int_D 2\,dx\wedge dy \amp = 2\operatorname{Area}(D) \amp = 2\pi.
So \boxed{\int_{\partial D}\omega=\int_D d\omega.}

The classical statement is \oint_C \mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r = \iint_D \left( \frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial y} \right) dA. The forms statement is \int_{\partial D}\omega=\int_D d\omega. They are the same calculation. The form notation simply keeps the integrand and the orientation together.

figure.caption: The boundary integral measures circulation. The exterior derivative measures circulation per unit area.

subsection.p:
Your task. Choose one classical vector calculus problem and translate it completely into forms. Use one of the following, or choose your own.
\begin{array}{c|c|c} \text{Classical problem} \amp \text{Classical theorem} \amp \text{Forms version} \\ \hline \oint_C \mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r \amp \text{Green's theorem} \amp \int_{\partial R}\omega=\int_R d\omega \\[1mm] \oint_{\partial S}\mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r \amp \text{Stokes' theorem} \amp \int_{\partial S}\omega=\int_S d\omega \\[1mm] \iint_{\partial E}\mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS \amp \text{Divergence theorem} \amp \int_{\partial E}\Phi=\int_E d\Phi \\[1mm] \int_C \nabla f\cdot d\mathbf r \amp \text{Fundamental theorem for line integrals} \amp \int_C df=\int_{\partial C}f \end{array}

li.p: State the classical problem.
Identify the correct form: \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz or \Phi=P\,dy\wedge dz+Q\,dz\wedge dx+R\,dx\wedge dy.
Compute the exterior derivative: d\omega \qquad\text{or}\qquad d\Phi.
Compute the integral directly, if possible.
Compute the integral using \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_Md\omega.
Write a translation table showing each classical object and its forms version.
Explain which notation made orientation easier to track.

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: the original classical problem,
the form version of the problem,
the calculation of d\omega or d\Phi,
both sides of the theorem,
a short translation table,
a paragraph explaining what the forms notation clarifies.

subsection.p: The best reports do not merely replace dots and crosses by wedges. They explain why the form is the natural integrand for the geometric object.

subsection.title: Project 2: closed but not exact forms on punctured domains
subsection.p: A 1-form \omega is called closed if d\omega=0. It is called exact if there is a function f such that \omega=df. Every exact form is closed, because d(df)=0. But the converse can fail when the domain has a hole.
This project is about that failure.
A seed example: the angular form. On the punctured plane \mathbb R^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}, define \boxed{\omega= \frac{-y\,dx+x\,dy}{x^2+y^2}.} This form measures change in angle around the origin.

Let P=\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad Q=\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}. Then \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy. Compute:
Q_x \amp = \frac{(x^2+y^2)-2x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{y^2-x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2},
and
P_y \amp = \frac{-(x^2+y^2)+2y^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{y^2-x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2}.
Thus Q_x-P_y=0. So \boxed{d\omega=0.} The form is closed.

If \omega were exact on the punctured plane, say \omega=df, then every closed-curve integral would be zero: \oint_C df=f(\text{endpoint})-f(\text{starting point})=0. But we found \oint_C\omega=2\pi. So \omega is closed but not exact on \mathbb R^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}.
The missing origin stores the obstruction.

figure.caption: A closed form can fail to be exact when the domain has a hole.

subsection.p: The second form is different from the angular form. In fact, \omega_2=d\left(\ln\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\right). So \omega_2 is exact on the punctured plane. Comparing \omega_1 and \omega_2 is a good way to see that closed and exact are not the same idea.

li.p: State the domain carefully. Do not include points where the formula is undefined.
Compute d\omega.
Decide whether the form is closed.
Integrate \omega around at least two closed curves: \text{one loop that surrounds the missing point} and \text{one loop that does not surround the missing point}.
Decide whether the form is exact on the whole domain.
If possible, find a potential function on a smaller domain, such as a plane with a ray removed.
Explain what the hole changes.
Why does d\omega=0 not automatically imply \omega=df?
What happens to the integral if a loop winds around the puncture twice?
Why does a loop that does not surround the puncture give a different kind of answer?
What is the role of the angle function \theta? Why can it be chosen locally but not globally around the puncture?
What does this example teach about the hypotheses in conservative-field theorems?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: the form and its domain,
the calculation of d\omega,
at least two closed-curve integrals,
a conclusion about closedness and exactness,
a drawing of the domain and the loops,
a paragraph explaining why the missing point matters.

subsection.p: The important lesson is not the formula itself. The important lesson is that local information can miss global topology. A form may have no local curl and still remember a hole.

subsection.title: Project 3: the generalized Stokes theorem on a cube
subsection.p: The generalized Stokes theorem says \boxed{\int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega.} For a solid cube, the form \omega must be a 2-form, because the boundary of a solid is a surface.
This project asks you to verify the theorem face by face.

A seed example: a flux form on the unit cube. Let B=[0,1]\times[0,1]\times[0,1] with orientation dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. Consider the 2-form \boxed{\Phi = x\,dy\wedge dz + y\,dz\wedge dx + z\,dx\wedge dy.} This is the flux form of the vector field \mathbf F=(x,y,z).

Now compute the boundary integral. The boundary has six faces. Using the outward orientation, the induced face orientations are:
\begin{array}{c|c} \text{Face} \amp \text{Induced orientation} \\ \hline x=1 \amp dy\wedge dz\\ x=0 \amp -dy\wedge dz\\ y=1 \amp dz\wedge dx\\ y=0 \amp -dz\wedge dx\\ z=1 \amp dx\wedge dy\\ z=0 \amp -dx\wedge dy \end{array}
On the face x=1, \Phi=1\,dy\wedge dz+\text{terms involving }dx. Terms involving dx vanish on this face because x is constant there. Hence \int_{x=1}\Phi=1.
On the face x=0, the coefficient x is zero, so the contribution is 0. Similarly, \int_{y=1}\Phi=1, \qquad \int_{y=0}\Phi=0, and \int_{z=1}\Phi=1, \qquad \int_{z=0}\Phi=0.

li.p: State the region and its orientation.
State the form \omega.
Compute d\omega.
Compute the interior integral: \int_M d\omega.
List all boundary pieces.
State the induced orientation on each boundary piece.
Compute each boundary contribution.
Add the boundary contributions and compare with the interior integral.
Which boundary pieces contribute zero? Why?
Where does orientation enter the calculation?
What would change if the region were given the opposite orientation?
In the three-dimensional case, what vector field has the chosen 2-form as its flux form?
In the two-dimensional case, what vector field has the chosen 1-form as its work form?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: a drawing of the region,
the form and its exterior derivative,
the interior integral,
a table of boundary pieces and orientations,
the boundary integral,
a sentence explaining how this example fits \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_Md\omega.

subsection.p: A cube is simple enough that no geometry is hidden. Every sign must be earned. That is why it is such a good testing ground for Stokes' theorem.

subsection.title: Project 4: orientation errors as sign errors
subsection.p: Many wrong answers in vector calculus are not calculus mistakes. They are orientation mistakes.
A curve is walked the wrong way. A normal vector points inward instead of outward. An inner boundary is treated like an outer boundary. A parametrization reverses the surface orientation.
Forms do not remove these errors, but they make them visible. A form changes sign when orientation reverses. If the sign is wrong, the geometry is usually wrong too.
The area is \pi(2^2)-\pi(1^2)=3\pi.

Now compute the boundary integral. On a counterclockwise circle of radius R, x=R\cos t,\qquad y=R\sin t. Then dx=-R\sin t\,dt, \qquad dy=R\cos t\,dt, so
\eta \amp = \frac12 \left( R\cos t(R\cos t\,dt) - R\sin t(-R\sin t\,dt) \right) \amp = \frac12R^2\,dt.
Therefore \int_{\text{ccw circle radius }R}\eta = \pi R^2.

The outer boundary of the annulus is oriented counterclockwise, so it contributes 4\pi. The inner boundary is oriented clockwise, so it contributes -\pi. Thus \boxed{\int_{\partial A}\eta=4\pi-\pi=3\pi.}
If we accidentally orient the inner boundary counterclockwise, we get 4\pi+\pi=5\pi. That answer is not the area of the annulus. The sign error has added the hole instead of subtracting it.

figure.caption: An inner boundary is positively oriented clockwise, because the region must stay on the left.

li.p: State the region, curve, or surface.
State its correct orientation.
Compute the integral correctly.
Deliberately make one orientation error.
Compute the incorrect result.
Explain exactly where the sign changed.
State a practical rule for avoiding that error.

subsection.p: What to hand in. Your report should include:

li.p: a drawing showing the correct orientation,
the correct calculation,
the incorrect calculation,
a comparison of the two answers,
a short sign diagnosis explaining the mistake,
a final checklist for future problems.

conclusion.p: These forms projects are computational, but they also train a habit of attention: integrals do not live on shapes alone. They live on oriented shapes. The last projects in the chapter ask for a different skill. After learning to compute, translate, and check signs, a mathematician has one more job: explain the story clearly enough that someone else can see why it matters.
